items.mistake: Assuming features are independent unconditionally (not given y)
items.fix: State the assumption correctly: xᵢ ⊥ xⱼ | y. Marginal correlations among features are allowed; only conditional independence given the class is assumed.
items.why: Confusing marginal and conditional independence leads to wrong diagnostic reasoning about when the model should work.
items.example: Email word counts correlate overall, but naive Bayes assumes independence **given spam/ham**—not that words are unrelated in the inbox.

items.mistake: Multiplying probabilities in raw space without logs
items.fix: Compute log P(y) + Σᵢ log P(xᵢ|y) and take argmax; use log-sum-exp if you need normalized posteriors.
items.why: Products of many probabilities underflow to zero in floating point, making all classes appear equally impossible.
items.example: 100 features each with P(xᵢ|y)≈0.5: raw product ≈ 8×10⁻³¹; log sum ≈ −100×log2 ≈ −69—still distinguishable between classes.

items.fix: For classification, compare unnormalized scores P(y)∏P(xᵢ|y) across classes; only compute P(x) when you need calibrated P(y|x).
items.why: P(x) is identical for every y in the argmax, so omitting it does not change the predicted label—but omitting P(y) does.
items.example: Two classes with equal products but P(spam)=0.9, P(ham)=0.1 → predict spam; dropping the prior flips the decision.

items.mistake: Treating P(xᵢ|y) as P(y|xᵢ) and deciding from one feature alone
items.fix: Apply Bayes over the full feature vector: multiply **all** P(xᵢ|y) factors (or their logs) and include P(y) before argmax.
items.why: Single-feature posteriors ignore evidence from other features and violate the joint model—even under independence.
items.example: P(spam|"FREE") alone is misleading; combine "FREE", "!!!", and other tokens via ∏ P(xᵢ|spam) × P(spam).
